message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "CLI: claim references item not in policy (amulet, only sword insured) → non-zero exit + stderr"
Expected behavior: when a claim step's incident has a damage whose itemType is not present in the referenced policy's insured items (e.g. amulet damage when only a sword is insured, or an unknown type), the CLI must write an error to stderr and exit non-zero, without writing results to stdout.
Implementation approach: in the CLI claim branch, before calling claim(), validate that every damage.itemType appears among the policy items' types. If any damage references a type not in the policy, throw/handle as an error: console.error a description and process.exit(1). Keep all 40 existing tests green.
Current error: expected status non-zero, received 0 (CLI doesn't validate damage itemTypes against policy).
Implementation file: src/cli.ts

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

36 R | sword (cap 2000) first claim 1500 → payout 1400 remainingCap 600 | comp:OK | run:OK | no compile error (claim+types exist, esbuild strips types, consistent with cycles 24-35); single-claim path max(0,1500-100)=1400 < cap 2000, remainingCap 2000-1400=600 already correct, test PASSES on activation (unavoidable, as predicted); payout-capping only matters for next test

content.text: Now let me read the failing test to understand exactly what's expected.

input.pattern: not in policy|amulet, only sword|claim references item

content.text:
The test sends a claim with a damage `itemType: "amulet"` against a policy that only insures a `sword`. I need to validate, before calling `claim()`, that every `damage.itemType` exists among the policy items' types.

The minimal change: in the claim branch, check each `step.incident.damages[].itemType` against the set of types in `items`. If any is missing, write to stderr and exit non-zero.

content.text:
All 41 tests pass (2 todo remaining). Let me verify minimality and that I didn't generalize beyond what the test demands.

The implementation only validates that each damage's `itemType` exists among the policy's insured types. It does not handle damage amount validation (next todo: negative amount) or count-of-damages validation (todo after that). This is exactly scoped to the active test.

Now I append to the journal.

input.command: printf '40 G | CLI claim branch: built insuredTypes set from policy items, reject any damage.itemType not in set via stderr+exit(1) before claim() | passing:41\n' >> /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_12-26-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/tdd-journal.md

content.text:
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: In the CLI claim branch (`src/cli.ts`), before calling `claim()`, I build a `Set` of the policy's insured item types from `items`, then iterate `step.incident.damages` and reject any whose `itemType` is not in that set — writing a message to stderr and calling `process.exit(1)`.
**Result**: All tests now pass (41 passing, 2 todo).
**Approach**: This is minimal — it validates only the one dimension the active test exercises (damage itemType vs. insured types). It does not touch damage-amount validation or damage-count validation (the two remaining `it.todo()` tests), so it won't preempt their Red phases. The guard runs before any results are written, satisfying the non-zero-exit + stderr + no-`results`-on-stdout assertions.

Proceeding to Refactor phase.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_12-26-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/cli.ts`
